summary:
The Second Saudi State (1824–1891) was the revival of the Saudi-Wahhabi political and religious project in central Arabia following the destruction of the First Saudi State by an Ottoman-Egyptian military expedition under Muhammad Ali in 1818. It demonstrated the resilience of the alliance between the House of Saud and Wahhabi Islamic reformism and provided the historical bridge between the first and the third (modern) Saudi states.

Turki ibn Abdallah reconstituted Saudi power in the Nejd region by 1824, recapturing Riyadh and making it the new capital. The Second State expanded to control much of the Arabian Peninsula's interior and maintained the strict Wahhabi religious interpretation that characterized Saudi rule — enforcing conservative Islamic practice, opposing what Wahhabis regarded as idolatrous innovations, and governing through a combination of tribal politics and religious authority.

The Second State was marked by internal dynastic conflicts that ultimately proved fatal. Succession disputes and family violence weakened central authority progressively from the 1860s onward. The rival Al Rashid dynasty of the Shammar tribe, based in Hail and backed by the Ottoman Empire, expanded its power at Saudi expense. After a series of defeats, the last Saudi ruler Abdurrahman ibn Faisal was driven from Riyadh in 1891, taking refuge with the Murra tribe in the Empty Quarter desert.

The Saudi revival would come a decade later when Abdurrahman's son, the young Abdulaziz ibn Saud (Ibn Saud), recaptured Riyadh in 1902 and began building the Third Saudi State that would eventually become the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The Wahhabi religious tradition maintained through both Saudi states has had enormous influence on global Islam through Saudi Arabia's wealth and missionary activity in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.
